subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Completed some college coursework, no degree attained
Coursework included introductory classes in information science and English literature
Participated in library orientation and training workshops provided by employer
Attended basic training on customer service and information management

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Library Assistant (Clerical)
Responsible for supporting daily library operations and user services
Role includes cataloguing, shelving, and circulation support
Acts as a first point of contact at the library front desk

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the education sector, specifically within an academic library setting
Supports faculty, staff, and student users
Library serves as a resource hub for research and study
Part of the broader information services industry

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed at a medium-sized academic institution
Library staff includes approximately 10-20 employees and several part-time student workers
Work environment is organized, quiet, and service-oriented
Frequent interaction with both small teams and individual library users

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Academic library serving higher education students and faculty
Focuses on providing access to print and digital scholarly resources
Offers programming such as research workshops and reading events
Supports both on-site and remote users

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience as a Library Assistant
Began working in the role at age 18 or 19
Has completed multiple academic terms in this position

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Entry-level to junior professional within the library team
Reports to a senior librarian or library supervisor
Frequently collaborates with other library assistants and clerical staff

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Assists students and staff in locating and using library resources
Catalogues and shelves books and materials according to classification systems
Maintains accurate circulation records for borrowed and returned items
Provides front-desk support, including issuing library cards and answering user questions
Supports inventory management and helps organize library events and displays

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends about 30% of time on cataloguing and shelving materials
Roughly 25% of time is dedicated to front-desk support and user assistance
Inventory checks and stock management account for about 10% of work hours
Event and program support typically takes up 5-10% of time, varying by semester
Remaining time is spent on record-keeping, reporting, and maintaining library order

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Operates under established library guidelines and procedures
Makes routine decisions about shelving, cataloguing, and user assistance independently
Refers complex issues or policy exceptions to a supervisor or senior librarian
Has some autonomy to resolve basic user problems and resource location queries

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Shelving books and materials according to Dewey Decimal or Library of Congress classification feels routine
Assisting users in using the OPAC system is comfortable due to frequent repetition
Issuing and renewing library cards is straightforward and follows clear protocol
Maintaining a quiet and orderly environment comes naturally

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Handling difficult or frustrated users requires tact and patience
Resolving discrepancies during inventory checks can be time-consuming
Learning to troubleshoot issues with the Library Management System presents a challenge
Supporting digital database searches for advanced academic topics may require more experience

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Data entry for cataloguing new materials is highly repetitive
Regular shelf-reading and inventory checks involve scanning and logging large amounts of data
Checking in and out materials using barcode or RFID is a repeatable process
Updating user account records could be automated for efficiency

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys helping students and staff find the resources they need
Organizing and setting up library events or displays is engaging and creative
Finds routine cataloguing and data entry to be less stimulating
Repetitive shelf-reading can feel tedious after prolonged periods

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual data entry for cataloguing is time-consuming and prone to minor errors
OPAC search interface can be unintuitive for new users, resulting in frequent assistance requests
Physical inventory checks are labor-intensive and can disrupt regular library flow
Occasional software slowdowns or outages in the LMS hinder productivity

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Feels most productive and satisfied when assisting users directly
Skill level is higher for frequently performed tasks like circulation and shelving, leading to increased confidence
Enjoyment and productivity both decline during repetitive administrative tasks
Tends to learn quickest on tasks that are both engaging and challenging

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Generally positive and curious about adopting new technologies
Views tech learning as essential to staying effective in the role
Enjoys experimenting with new digital tools that improve workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to updates in library management and cataloguing systems
Able to pick up new digital platforms with minimal instruction
Occasional need for step-by-step guidance when systems are complex

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate several hours per month to tech training sessions
Frequently seeks out online tutorials or peer advice to troubleshoot tech issues
Prefers hands-on, practical training over lengthy theoretical courses

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the potential for increased efficiency and reduced manual work
Finds learning relevant when new tech directly improves daily tasks
Heavy workload during peak academic periods can limit time for training
May feel less motivated if new tools seem tangential to core responsibilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers often share tips and recommendations for useful software, fostering a collaborative learning environment
Management encouragement and demonstrations increase willingness to try new tools
Positive feedback from supervisors after adopting new tech reinforces learning

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Koha Library Management System for cataloguing and circulation
Relies on Online Public Access Catalog (OPAC) for assisting user searches
Operates barcode scanners for material check-in and check-out
Regularly accesses Microsoft Word and Excel for record-keeping and reports

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Manual shelving carts and bookends used for organizing materials
Library security gates to monitor material movement
Physical inventory checklists during stock-taking
Labels and call number stickers for classification

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Comfortable with major academic databases such as JSTOR and EBSCOhost
Familiar with RFID systems for inventory and security, though not always available
Understands the basics of digital repositories for theses and publications

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasional lag when exporting records from LMS to Excel for reporting
Some incompatibility between older barcode scanners and the latest LMS updates
Manual data reconciliation needed when digital repository and cataloguing data do not sync automatically

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands the basics of machine learning, natural language processing, and large language models (LLMs)
Can explain concepts like AI chatbots, recommendation systems, and automated classification
Comfortable using AI-driven search and summarization tools in daily work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Regularly uses ChatGPT or similar LLMs for drafting communication and answering user queries
Employs AI-powered cataloguing features within the LMS
Occasionally uses AI-driven citation and reference management tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs daily to help answer complex or unusual reference questions from users
Applies AI to automate initial drafts of event descriptions and program announcements
Relies on AI for generating summaries of lengthy academic resources for users
Employs AI-powered search to locate hard-to-find materials in digital repositories

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI chatbots have quickly resolved common user inquiries, reducing wait times
Automated cataloguing suggestions have improved classification speed but occasionally misclassify niche materials
AI-generated summaries saved time but sometimes missed key context, requiring manual review
Learned to always double-check AI outputs for accuracy on important tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Received brief in-house training on using LLMs and AI features in the LMS
Peers frequently share tips and troubleshooting strategies for AI tools
No formal certification but access to online tutorials and knowledge base articles

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers starting independently with AI tools, then requesting step-by-step guidance as needed
Occasionally collaborates with peers to interpret or refine AI outputs
Values the flexibility to switch between autonomous and guided interaction

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an efficient digital assistant for routine tasks
Sometimes treats AI as an advisor when seeking recommendations or summaries
Does not rely on AI as a full co-worker but as a support tool for specific processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to integrating new AI features that demonstrably improve efficiency
Willing to try pilot programs or beta features introduced by the library
Prefers gradual adoption with clear guidance and fallback options

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Finds conversational interfaces like chatbots intuitive and user-friendly
Uses command-based interfaces for specific, repetitive actions in the LMS
Prefers conversational AI for problem-solving and knowledge queries

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many user assistance tasks require understanding library layout and collection details
Some cataloguing decisions benefit from knowledge of academic disciplines
AI can automate basic classification, but nuanced resource recommendations still need human judgment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI with routine queries and initial drafts, but verifies results for critical user requests
Has moderate confidence in AI-powered cataloguing, subject to manual review
Reserves final decisions for human staff, especially with unusual or sensitive materials

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI automation for repetitive data entry and cataloguing
Values human involvement in user interaction and complex problem-solving
Believes hybrid workflows leveraging both AI speed and human nuance are optimal

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for background processes with minimal user impact
Feels automation is threatening if it reduces opportunities for meaningful user engagement
Supports automation as long as manual override and review options are available

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances need for digital literacy and information management skills
Manual cataloguing and data entry skills may become less central
Customer service and critical thinking remain vital and are less affected by automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Learning to evaluate and interpret AI-generated outputs is an emerging skill
Gains responsibility for training peers in AI tool usage
Develops skills in troubleshooting and customizing AI workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Moderately concerned about automation of repetitive clerical tasks such as cataloguing
Believes core functions like user support and event organization are less likely to be automated
Sees AI as a complement rather than a replacement for the overall role

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Manual review is always performed for AI-generated catalogue entries
Staff can override AI recommendations in the LMS when necessary
Supervisors periodically audit AI-affected workflows for accuracy

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Policies have gradually shifted toward encouraging AI experimentation
Team meetings now include regular updates on AI tool performance
Management seeks staff feedback before fully integrating new AI features

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Views AI as a transformative force in information management and research support
Believes AI will improve access to knowledge and reduce administrative burden in libraries
Acknowledges potential for job displacement in repetitive roles, but sees new opportunities emerging

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports transparent AI systems with clear accountability mechanisms
Worries about potential biases in AI-driven recommendations and search results
Advocates for ongoing human oversight to prevent unintended consequences

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would benefit from smarter natural language search that understands context and intent
Seeks AI tools for automated event planning and scheduling
Desires enhanced AI-driven accessibility features for users with disabilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Anticipates more time spent on user engagement and less on manual cataloguing
Foresees AI handling most routine data management tasks
Expects to play a larger role in training and supervising AI-supported workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Advocates for regular staff training on new AI tools and updates
Recommends transparent communication about AI adoption plans and impacts
Wants structured feedback channels for staff to report issues or suggest improvements to AI systems
